summary: The daf opens with the paze"r kashe"v mnemonic that encapsulates Shemini Atzeret's six points of independence from Sukkot - though for compensation offerings it still counts as Sukkot's final day. The Gemara then systematically excludes the remaining Festival candidates from the gezerah shavah: Shavuot is ruled out because its mussaf includes two rams rather than one, and even by R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's count the two rams serve different purposes. Rosh Hashana is excluded because the bull and ram on Yom Kippur must come from the Kohen Gadol's personal funds, while those on Rosh Hashana and Shavuot come from community property. The daf closes mid-discussion with the unresolved question of how to handle the opinion that 'kach lecha' and 'aseh lecha' both mean communal property.

display.title: Paze"r Kashe"v: Shemini Atzeret's six points of independence
display.oneLine: The mnemonic paze"r kashe"v lists six ways Shemini Atzeret is independent of Sukkot, yet for compensation offerings it still counts as the final day of Sukkot - which is precisely why it was excluded from the gezerah shavah.
display.shortSummary: Rashi from 2b continues with Rav Ashi's argument, and the Gemara then presents the mnemonic summarizing Shemini Atzeret's six-fold independence: its own lottery, shehecheyanu blessing, festival status, mussaf count, Levitical song, and prayer addition. Despite all six, Shemini Atzeret still counts as the last day of Sukkot for tashlumin (compensation offerings) - confirming it is preceded by holy days and cannot be the source of the separation rule.
display.whats: The mnemonic paze"r kashe"v lists six respects in which Shemini Atzeret is treated as an independent Festival: its own lottery for priests, its own shehecheyanu blessing, its own Festival status, its own korban count, its own Levitical song, and its own prayer addition. Yet when it comes to tashlumin - compensation for a missed Festival offering - Shemini Atzeret is still counted as the last day of Sukkot. This dual status (independent in most ways, yet an extension for tashlumin) is what disqualifies it from the gezerah shavah: Shemini Atzeret is preceded by seven holy days, not by ordinary weekdays.
display.hint: Why does tashlumin status not contradict Shemini Atzeret's independence in the other six respects?

learning.learnerQuestion: If Shemini Atzeret is independent in six different ways, why does it still count as part of Sukkot for tashlumin purposes?
learning.coreTension: The six-fold independence is real - but it coexists with a subordinate relationship in one specific legal category (tashlumin). How can a single holiday be both independent and dependent?
learning.coreMove: The mnemonic itself is the Gemara's evidence: the six independencies are listed to show they are the exception, not the rule. For most purposes Shemini Atzeret depends on Sukkot - and specifically, it is preceded by Sukkot's holy days.
learning.resolution: The six points of independence are each specific legal formalities (lottery, blessing, etc.). They do not affect the fundamental sanctity relationship. Tashlumin status is a direct consequence of that sanctity relationship remaining intact.

takeaway.text: A holiday can have formal independence in specific legal domains while remaining fundamentally attached to its predecessor in others. Independence is not all-or-nothing.

reasoningPattern.notes: The mnemonic illustrates how domain-specific independence coexists with broader continuity.

learning.ahaMoment: The tashlumin rule is not a contradiction to the mnemonic - it is confirmation of what the mnemonic means. The six points are specified precisely because they are exceptions to a general rule of Sukkot-dependence. The exception list proves the rule.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think the six-fold independence means Shemini Atzeret is completely separate from Sukkot. The Gemara shows independence is domain-specific.
learning.memoryAnchor: The mnemonic lists six points of independence - but the seventh point (tashlumin) is the opposite: Shemini Atzeret is still subordinate to Sukkot for compensation purposes. The six exceptions prove the rule that it remains attached to Sukkot.

argumentFlow.label: Six points of independence
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Peh-zayin-reish, kuf-shin-beit: lottery (payis), shehecheyanu (zeman), festival-status (regel), offering-count (korban), Levitical-song (shira), prayer-addition (berakhah) - Shemini Atzeret is independent in all six.

argumentFlow.label: But for tashlumin
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: However, for compensation offerings, Shemini Atzeret counts as the last day of Sukkot - as the Mishnah in Sukkah teaches, one who missed the Festival offering may bring it through all the days of the Festival including the final Festival day.

misconceptions.misconception: The six points of independence mean Shemini Atzeret has no relationship to Sukkot.
misconceptions.correction: The six points are exceptions to a general rule of Sukkot-dependence. They are listed precisely because they are exceptions. Tashlumin status shows the underlying attachment remains intact.

visualizableElements.item: Table of paze"r kashe"v: six categories (lottery, blessing, festival, offering, song, prayer) each listed as independent, with tashlumin as the seventh category where Shemini Atzeret is subordinate
Timeline: seven days of Sukkot -> Shemini Atzeret (tashlumin window still open), showing the attachment that disqualifies it from the gezerah shavah

quizSeeds.question: What six respects does the paze"r kashe"v mnemonic list as Shemini Atzeret's independence from Sukkot?
quizSeeds.answer: Payis (its own priestly lottery), Zeman (its own shehecheyanu blessing), Regel (its own festival name), Korban (its own offering count), Shira (its own Levitical song), Berakhah (its own prayer addition).

quizSeeds.question: In what way does Shemini Atzeret still count as part of Sukkot, despite all six points of independence?
quizSeeds.answer: For tashlumin (compensation offerings): one who missed a Festival offering during Sukkot may bring it on any subsequent Sukkot day including Shemini Atzeret as the final day.

display.title: Excluding Shavuot via the bull-and-ram comparison
display.oneLine: Shavuot is excluded because its mussaf involves two rams while Yom Kippur and the inauguration each have one bull and one ram; and even R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's two-ram view doesn't help, because those two rams serve different purposes.
display.shortSummary: Rabbi Abba excludes Shavuot by matching offering profiles: Yom Kippur brings one bull and one ram, matching the inauguration; Shavuot brings two rams. A challenge is raised: R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon says Yom Kippur also involves two rams (one from Leviticus, one from Numbers). The Gemara resolves this: even on that view, the two Yom Kippur rams serve different functions (day-obligation vs. mussaf), while both Shavuot rams are day-obligations. So the structural mismatch remains.
display.whats: Shavuot is excluded by a bull-and-ram count: Yom Kippur's offering matches the inauguration's (one bull + one ram), while Shavuot brings two rams. Even R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon, who holds Yom Kippur uses two rams (one from Leviticus, one from Numbers), cannot use Shavuot as the source - because on Yom Kippur the two rams serve different functions (day-obligation vs. mussaf), while on Shavuot both are day-obligations. The structural difference blocks the analogy.
display.hint: The Gemara is careful to distinguish not just quantity but function of the offerings. Why does function matter for a gezerah shavah?

learning.learnerQuestion: Shavuot is preceded by weekdays and involves a one-day service. What specifically disqualifies it from the gezerah shavah?
learning.coreTension: Shavuot appears to match the structural criteria (one day, preceded by weekdays), but its offering profile doesn't match. Is the offering count a formal criterion or a hint to an underlying functional distinction?
learning.coreMove: R. Abba's argument: the gezerah shavah derives from offering similarity (one bull and one ram), not just temporal structure. R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's challenge is met by shifting from quantity to function.
learning.resolution: Even with two rams on Yom Kippur, those rams serve categorically different purposes (day-obligation vs. additional offering). Shavuot's two rams both fulfill the same type of obligation. The functional asymmetry blocks the analogy.

takeaway.text: An analogy must match not only surface features (count of offerings) but also functional structure (what role each offering plays). Matching in one dimension while mismatching in another blocks the derivation.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Gemara extends the matching criteria from quantity to function.

learning.ahaMoment: The two-ram question is a trap that reveals something deeper: quantity is only the proxy for function. Once you look at function (chovat hayom vs. mussaf), the mismatch is clear regardless of whether Yom Kippur has one or two rams.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's view devastates R. Abba's argument. The Gemara shows it doesn't, because the argument operates at the functional level.
learning.memoryAnchor: Yom Kippur: one bull and one ram. Inauguration: one bull and one ram. Shavuot: one bull and two rams. Even if Yom Kippur has two rams (R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon), those two rams serve different obligations; Shavuot's two rams serve the same obligation.

argumentFlow.label: Shavuot as candidate
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: And say that separation is required before Shavuot - it is also sequestering of seven days for one day, and preceded by weekdays.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Abba: offering count
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Abba
argumentFlow.text: We derive one bull and one ram (Yom Kippur) from one bull and one ram (inauguration) - to the exclusion of Shavuot, which brings two rams.

argumentFlow.label: What if Yom Kippur has two rams?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: This works well for the view that Yom Kippur has one ram. But for R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon, who holds there are two rams on Yom Kippur, what is there to say?

sourceRefs.note: Challenge: R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon

argumentFlow.label: Different functions
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Even on R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's view: on Yom Kippur, one ram is the day's obligation and the other is the mussaf. This excludes Shavuot, where both rams are day-obligations.

misconceptions.misconception: R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's view (two rams on YK) defeats R. Abba's argument.
misconceptions.correction: Even on R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's count, the two Yom Kippur rams serve different purposes (chovat hayom vs. mussaf). Shavuot's two rams both fulfill the same obligation. Quantity was only a proxy for function.

visualizableElements.item: Offering profile comparison table: inauguration (1 bull + 1 ram), YK (1 bull + 1 ram), Shavuot (1 bull + 2 rams) - the mismatch is clear at a glance
R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon variant: YK's two rams split into chovat hayom (day-obligation) and mussaf (additional), vs. Shavuot's two rams both as chovat hayom - function differs even when count matches

quizSeeds.question: How does R. Abba use the offering profile to exclude Shavuot from the gezerah shavah?
quizSeeds.answer: Yom Kippur and the inauguration both bring one bull and one ram. Shavuot brings one bull and two rams. We derive one bull plus one ram from one bull plus one ram, to the exclusion of Shavuot.

quizSeeds.question: R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon holds Yom Kippur has two rams. Why doesn't this restore Shavuot's candidacy?
quizSeeds.answer: On Yom Kippur, the two rams serve different functions: one is the day's obligation (chovat hayom) and the other is the mussaf addition. On Shavuot, both rams are day-obligations. The functional mismatch persists even when the count matches.

display.title: Excluding Rosh Hashana via the personal-property requirement
display.oneLine: Rosh Hashana is excluded because its bull and ram come from communal funds, while Yom Kippur's and the inauguration's come from the kohen's personal property - the daf closes open on this point.
display.shortSummary: Rabbi Abbahu excludes Rosh Hashana and Shavuot by a new criterion: the Yom Kippur and inauguration bulls and rams come from the High Priest's own funds ('take for yourself'), while the Rosh Hashana and Shavuot offerings come from the community. The Gemara then raises a challenge: this works according to the view that 'kach lecha' means personal property - but what about the view that 'aseh lecha' means personal property too? The daf ends mid-discussion.
display.whats: The final candidate is Rosh Hashana: preceded by ordinary days, and both it and the inauguration involve a bull and a ram. R. Abbahu's answer is the personal-property dimension: on Yom Kippur and at the inauguration, the Kohen Gadol brings these offerings from his own funds. The Rosh Hashana and Shavuot offerings come from communal property. The gezerah shavah links cases where the animal comes from the Kohen's personal wealth. The page ends with this argument open - the next page addresses the view that 'kach lecha' does not mean personal property.
display.hint: Personal property vs. communal property might seem like a minor detail. Why does R. Abbahu treat it as definitive for the gezerah shavah?

learning.learnerQuestion: Rosh Hashana is preceded by ordinary weekdays and involves a one-day service. What finally excludes it?
learning.coreTension: Rosh Hashana seems to meet all the criteria established so far. R. Abbahu introduces a new criterion - personal vs. communal property - that the Gemara has not used before. Is this a new principle or a derivation from the text?
learning.coreMove: 'Take for yourself' in Leviticus 9 (inauguration) and Leviticus 16 (Yom Kippur) both imply personal property. Rosh Hashana's offerings are explicitly communal. The analogy requires matching in this dimension too.
learning.resolution: The daf closes open. R. Abbahu's argument succeeds for the view that 'kach lecha' means personal property; a challenge is raised for an alternative view, and the resolution continues on 3b.

takeaway.text: The distinction between personal and communal funding of Temple offerings is legally significant - it affects the nature of the offering and its capacity to generate analogies. The Gemara is not yet done working out all the implications.

reasoningPattern.notes: A new criterion - personal vs. communal property source - is added to the analogy requirements.

learning.ahaMoment: Every earlier exclusion was about the structure of the service (before/after, frequency, duration, sanctity of preceding days). R. Abbahu shifts to the economics of the offering itself - whose property it is. This is a fundamentally different category of criterion.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may not understand why property source matters for a gezerah shavah. The link is that 'take for yourself' is a specific legal phrase indicating personal ownership, which is part of the inaugurational context being analyzed.
learning.memoryAnchor: The bull and ram on Yom Kippur and at the inauguration come from the High Priest's own pocket. Rosh Hashana's offerings are communal. Personal ownership is the final link in the analogy chain.

argumentFlow.label: Rosh Hashana as candidate
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: And say the separation is required before Rosh Hashana - also sequestering of seven days for one day, preceded by weekdays, with a bull and a ram.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Abbahu: personal property
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Abbahu
argumentFlow.text: We derive 'his bull and his ram' (personal property at inauguration) from 'his bull and his ram' (personal property on Yom Kippur) - to exclude Shavuot and Rosh Hashana, whose offerings are communal.

argumentFlow.label: Works for the view 'kach lecha' = personal
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: This works well according to the one who says 'take you [kach lecha]' means from your own property.

sourceRefs.note: Works for the 'kach lecha' = personal view

argumentFlow.label: Open: alternative view
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: [The daf closes mid-argument - the challenge to R. Abbahu's reasoning continues on 3b]

misconceptions.misconception: R. Abbahu introduces an arbitrary new criterion just to exclude Rosh Hashana.
misconceptions.correction: 'Kach lecha' is a specific legal phrase indicating personal ownership, genuinely present in both the inauguration and Yom Kippur texts. The challenge at the daf's end shows this criterion is contested, not arbitrary - it depends on how 'kach lecha' is read.

visualizableElements.item: Personal property diagram: 'kach lecha' in inauguration (Lev 9) and 'kach lecha' in YK (Lev 16) both point to Kohen Gadol's personal funds; RH and Shavuot use communal property

quizSeeds.question: What criterion does R. Abbahu use to exclude Rosh Hashana from the gezerah shavah?
quizSeeds.answer: Personal vs. communal property. The bull and ram on Yom Kippur and at the inauguration come from the Kohen Gadol's personal funds ('take for yourself - kach lecha'). Rosh Hashana's offerings are communal.

quizSeeds.question: Why does the page end without resolving R. Abbahu's argument?
quizSeeds.answer: A challenge is raised about an alternative view of 'kach lecha' - whether it means personal property or communal. The daf closes mid-argument and the resolution continues on 3b.

glossary.en: Mnemonic acronym listing the six ways Shemini Atzeret is independent of Sukkot: payis (its own lottery), zeman (its own shehecheyanu), regel (its own festival status), korban (its own offering count), shira (its own Levitical song), berakhah (its own prayer addition).

Compensation offering. If someone missed the obligatory Festival offering (chagigah) on the first day of Yom Tov, they may bring it on any subsequent day of that Yom Tov. Shemini Atzeret counts as a compensation day for Sukkot - the one way it remains linked to its predecessor festival.

The day-specific obligation - an offering required because of the special holiness of that particular day, as opposed to mussaf offerings which are additional to the day's regular service. The Gemara distinguishes Yom Kippur from Shavuot precisely on this point.

Used in the Talmud for both Shavuot and Shemini Atzeret - the word means 'gathering' or 'assembly.' Context determines which holiday is meant. The shared term reflects their common structure as single-day culminating celebrations at the end of longer festival sequences.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi introduces the topic of paze"r kashe"v: the first point, 'payis for itself,' means Shemini Atzeret has its own lottery for priestly service, distinct from the bulls-of-Sukkot lottery where all the priestly watches rotate and take turns.
Rashi continues: all the priestly watches vary and rotate for the Sukkot bulls according to the schedule of their assigned order,
as we learned in tractate Sukkah (55b) - but the bull of Shemini Atzeret: at the outset a new lottery is held specifically for it.

Rashi glosses the remaining items of the mnemonic: 'zeman for itself' means the shehecheyanu blessing is recited on Shemini Atzeret; 'regel for itself' means the name 'Festival of Sukkot' does not apply to it.
Rashi continues with 'korban for itself': Shemini Atzeret's offering count
is not equal to all the other days of the festival
- which all have many bulls, two rams,
and fourteen lambs - whereas Shemini Atzeret has: one bull, one ram,
and seven lambs. 'Shira for itself': Shemini Atzeret's Levitical song
is not the same as the songs of all the other days of the festival.
All the songs of the festival days deal with the theme of 'gazal' -
charitable gifts to the poor - and those of Chol HaMoed
are specified in tractate Sukkah (55b) as specific Psalms for each day.
But the Psalm of Shemini Atzeret does not follow the 'harvest' theme of the rest of Sukkot
and its specific Psalm has not been identified for us. 'Berakhah for itself':
the 'blessing' refers to the special prayer they would recite for the welfare of the king,
as explained in the Tosefta (ch. 4 of Sukkah),
based on the verse 'On the eighth day he sent the people away and they blessed the king' (I Kings 8).

Rashi now addresses the tashlumin clause: 'compensation for the first [Festival] it is' - this shows Shemini Atzeret is still subordinate to Sukkot for purposes of making up a missed Festival offering.
Rashi turns to the gezerah shavah argument: 'One derives one bull and one ram [from one bull and one ram]' -
the mussaf service of Yom Kippur involves one bull and one ram; the inauguration service on the eighth day also involved
one bull and one ram - the eighth day of the inauguration had: one bull and one ram (the mussaf of that day),
one bull and one ram as written in Leviticus 9:
'Take for yourself a bull calf for a sin-offering, etc.' - establishing the offering profile of the inauguration.
[Colon indicating continuation of Rashi's citation]
Rashi explains the view that Yom Kippur has 'one ram': that communal offering
on Yom Kippur - the ram mentioned in Acharei Mot (Leviticus 16) - is the same one
as the one mentioned in the book of Numbers (Pinchas section): 'From the congregation of Israel he shall take two goats
for a sin-offering and one ram for a burnt-offering' - it is the same ram mentioned in Pinchas for the mussaf; therefore Yom Kippur has only one ram.
Rashi explains R. Eliezer b. R. Shimon's resolution: one [ram] is for the day's obligation (chovat hayom) - the Acharei Mot ram is the day's obligation, not called mussaf,

and the phrase 'besides the morning burnt-offering' is not written there, as it is written for all the mussaf offerings - 'besides the morning burnt-offering' indicates something added on top of the daily offering.
To exclude Shavuot: both its rams are day-obligations (chovat hayom) -
both Shavuot rams are linked to the day's obligation of the Two Loaves. As written in Leviticus 23:
'And you shall offer upon the bread ... and two rams' - whereas the day's obligation at the inauguration was only one ram.
Rashi now presents R. Abbahu's argument: 'One derives his bull [i.e., his own]' - the inauguration's bull came from the kohen's own property, as written 'take for yourself a calf'
and Yom Kippur also has a bull and ram from the High Priest's own property - 'In this shall Aaron come to the Sanctuary: with a bull
son of cattle etc.' - to exclude Shavuot and Rosh Hashana, whose bulls and rams come from communal funds.
Rashi's final word on 3a: 've-aseh' (and make) - the continuation of the Gemara's challenge regarding 'aseh lecha' (make for yourself), which continues on 3b.
